# gated-review

Bounded MCP server for agent-driven GitHub PR reviews, with enforced triage and merge gates.

## Standards

<!-- import: /home/steve/codes/rol/catagents/standards/core.md @ 1362c01763c6 -->
## Two gates before building

**Stop when uncertain.** Before introducing a pattern, dependency, file, or structural
decision with no precedent in these standards or the existing codebase — stop and ask. A
wrong foundational choice is expensive to unwind; a question is cheap. This applies
especially to destructive file operations, schema and data-model decisions, new
dependencies, and changes to public interfaces.

**Data model before implementation.** No feature work begins until the domain model
exists as named types, derived from the real data source — DB schema, API contract, CLI
output, upstream types. The model is not invented to fit code as it emerges. If you
cannot point at the source the model mirrors, you do not have a model yet. Improvised
data shapes metastasize: every consumer invents its own slice, arguments proliferate,
state scatters.

## Pipelines as the default

Express logic as composable pipelines of small named transforms — each takes typed
input, returns typed output. If you cannot draw the data flow as a graph of typed nodes,
rethink the design.

- Each transform is independently testable — pure or pure-ish, reusable across flows.
- Pipelines are fractal: a node can itself be a pipeline; a cycle is a nested subgraph.
- Use a loop when it is genuinely clearer — early break, side effects, complex
  accumulation. Outside those three cases, default to the pipeline.

## Errors as values

A `Result` type (`Ok | Err`) is the connecting contract between pipeline stages. Every
fallible transform returns `Result`; the caller pattern-matches. Domain error types
carry trace context — operation name, entity id, detail — so a failure deep in a flow
can be traced to its path. `try`/`catch` lives only at IO boundaries: catch there,
convert to `Result`, hand it to the pipeline. (Concrete `Result` type: see the language
fragment.)

## Local primitives

Roll small primitives locally — `Result`, domain error types, branded ids — rather than
pull a library. They are a dozen lines, do not change, and importing them as a dependency
binds the project to someone else's release cadence for nothing in return. The language
fragments include drop-in definitions.

## Option handling

Represent absence with the language's nullable (`T | None`, `Option<T>`) — never sentinel
strings, magic numbers, or empty collections as a stand-in for "not there". When two or
more values can be absent, prefer a chain of early-return guards over nested
conditionals; flat reads better than pyramidal.

## Derived values are transforms

A derived value is a pure named function of typed input. Call it a **selector** in UI
code, a **transform** everywhere else — same idea, named for its setting. If a derivation
appears in two or more places, extract and name it. Consumers read derived values; they
never re-derive inline.

## Typing discipline

- Structured data always has a named type. Never a raw dict / untyped object /
  `Record<string, unknown>` for a known shape.
- A finite set of variants is a discriminated union — a tag plus a typed payload per
  variant — never a bare string tag beside an untyped bag.
- Domain identifiers get nominal/branded types (`UserId`, `OrderId`), not bare strings.

## Arguments

Three or more semantically related arguments → group them into one named typed object.
Long pass-through argument lists — arguments a function takes only to forward — are a
layering smell, not a formatting one: before grouping them, ask whether the intermediate
function should exist at all.

## Testing

Test behavior, not implementation — tests should survive refactors. Pure
transforms/selectors are the unit-test surface; side-effecting and IO code is tested at
its boundary. One clear assertion per test where practical; descriptive names.

## Naming

`snake_case` / `PascalCase` / `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE` per the language's norm. Descriptive
over terse — `user_count`, not `n`. Booleans read as questions — `is_active`,
`has_permission`, `should_retry`.
<!-- /import: /home/steve/codes/rol/catagents/standards/core.md -->

<!-- import: /home/steve/codes/rol/catagents/standards/typescript.md @ 1362c01763c6 -->
## Strictness

`strict` mode on. No `any` — use `unknown` and narrow. No non-null assertions (`!`)
without a documented reason. A clean compile is a merge gate.

## Types

- A finite set of variants is a discriminated union — a literal tag plus a typed
  payload — never a string tag beside an untyped payload.
- Domain identifiers get branded types, not bare strings:

```ts
type UserId = string & { readonly __brand: 'UserId' }
```

- Never `Record<string, unknown>` (or `any`) for a shape that is known — name it.
- One definition per type. A type used on two sides lives in a shared module both
  import, never duplicated and hand-synced.

## Result

A project-local `Result<T, E>` discriminated union:

```ts
type Result<T, E> = { ok: true; value: T } | { ok: false; error: E }
```

Fallible functions return it; callers narrow on `ok`. Exceptions are for IO boundaries
only.

## Modules

Explicit named exports — avoid default exports. Absolute imports from the project root.
No re-export barrels that obscure where a symbol is defined.

## Tooling

The project's configured linter and formatter (ESLint + Prettier, or Biome) clean.
Type-check in CI, not just locally.
<!-- /import: /home/steve/codes/rol/catagents/standards/typescript.md -->

<!-- import: /home/steve/codes/rol/catagents/standards/backend.md @ 1362c01763c6 -->
## Contracts first

Define the API or data contract as named types before writing the handler. Derive the
contract from the real schema or upstream source — do not let it emerge from the
implementation.

## IO boundaries

`try`/`catch` lives only at IO edges — network, disk, database, external processes. Catch
there, convert the failure to a `Result`, and hand it to the pipeline. Interior logic
deals in `Result`, never exceptions.

## Logging is a prerequisite

Every significant decision, state transition, and rejection is logged with enough context
to reconstruct the path that produced it. If a system is too heavy to log its decisions,
it is too heavy to run unsupervised — that is the test for whether a flow is ready, not
an afterthought.

## Persistence

Database access goes through a typed repository layer — never raw queries scattered
through handlers. Mutations are idempotent wherever the domain allows it. The persistence
schema is a defined model, not an implicit shape.

## Errors

Domain error types carry operation name, entity id, and detail, so a trace can
reconstruct where and why a failure occurred. Errors are values returned, not control
flow thrown across layers.

## Configuration and secrets

Configuration is a typed, validated object, checked at startup — fail fast on a bad
config. Secrets come from the environment; never commit them, never log them.

## Backend testing

Pure logic is unit-tested in isolation. IO and integration seams are tested at the
boundary, against real or faithfully-faked dependencies.
<!-- /import: /home/steve/codes/rol/catagents/standards/backend.md -->

## Environment

<!-- import: /home/steve/codes/rol/catagents/standards/gated-review.md @ 1362c01763c6 -->
## Gated Review environment

This environment has the gated-review MCP server connected. All PR and
remote-git operations go through it — never the shell or the GitHub API.

**Never use:**
- `gh` (GitHub CLI) for any PR or API operation
- `git push`, `git pull`, or `git fetch` in the shell
- the GitHub REST or GraphQL API directly

**Always use instead:**
- `get_review_round` — read PR review threads and comments
- `reply_to_thread` — reply to a review thread
- `resolve_thread` — resolve a handled thread
- `git.push` / `git.pull` / `git.fetch` — all remote git operations

**Merging and merge-ready are human-only.** Do not call `merge_pr` or
`mark_merge_ready`, and do not decide merge readiness. Report the evidence
(open thread count, push status) and hand the decision to the operator.
<!-- /import: /home/steve/codes/rol/catagents/standards/gated-review.md -->
